common_name: Sodom Apple (Rubber Bush, Dead Sea Apple)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy dryland soil in full sun; Sodom apple is a drought-resistant tropical shrub native to Africa and the Middle East — producing distinctive, large, hollow, inflated seed pods resembling smooth apples that contain silky, white, cottony fiber around the seeds; ALL parts are toxic — the milky latex causes severe skin and eye irritation; excellent for xeriscape and dryland ornamental plantings in zone 9–12.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy dryland soil in full sun; extremely drought-tolerant; CAUTION: All parts are highly toxic; the milky latex causes severe skin and eye irritation — wear gloves and eye protection when handling. Calotropis procera (Sodom apple, rubber bush, or Dead Sea apple) is a drought-resistant tropical shrub native to the dryland regions of sub-Saharan Africa, the Sahel, North Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia — naturalized across warm, arid, disturbed habitats worldwide. The plant is related to and resembles crown flower (C. gigantea) but is typically smaller and better adapted to drier, harsher conditions. The most distinctive feature of C. procera is its unusual fruit: large (2–3 inch), perfectly round, hollow, inflated pods that look like smooth, apple-green, apple-shaped balloons — hence 'Sodom apple' (the reference to Sodom in the Dead Sea region where it is found). When ripe and dry, the pods burst open to release small seeds attached to long, silky, white, cottony fibers similar to milkweed floss; this floss has been used as stuffing material and in traditional rope-making. Small (1-inch), waxy, lavender and white, star-shaped flowers are produced abundantly. The milky white latex is extremely toxic and caustic — used historically as an arrow poison in Africa. Despite its toxicity, the fiber and bark have traditional uses across Africa and Asia.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calotropis species
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Red Porterweed (Cayenne Porterweed, Light Blue Snakeweed)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Cayenne porterweed is a distinct Stachytarpheta species closely related to the more common blue porterweed (S. jamaicensis) — producing lighter blue, smaller flowers on slender, erect spikes, with similar extreme value as a butterfly nectar plant; fast-growing, drought-tolerant, and blooms nearly year-round in zone 9–12 tropical landscapes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; very drought-tolerant once established; self-sows freely in warm climates. Stachytarpheta cayennensis (Cayenne porterweed or light blue snakeweed) is a tropical perennial shrub native to the Caribbean, tropical Americas, and Central America — closely related to the widely-grown blue porterweed (S. jamaicensis) and sharing its exceptional ecological value as a butterfly nectar plant. Like S. jamaicensis, it produces long, slender, erect spikes with small, individual flowers that open one at a time along the spike over an extended period — but the flowers of S. cayennensis are lighter blue to lavender-blue and slightly smaller than those of S. jamaicensis. Both species are among the highest-value plants in the tropical butterfly garden: the elongated flower spike design provides ideal perches for feeding butterflies, and the plant blooms nearly continuously in warm climates. Like S. jamaicensis, S. cayennensis is also used medicinally in tropical traditional medicine across its range — as an anti-inflammatory, antifungal, and general tonic herb in Caribbean and Latin American folk medicine. The two species are nearly identical in garden use and can be distinguished mainly by flower size and shade intensity. Often naturalized as a weed in disturbed tropical habitats worldwide.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Stachytarpheta species
North American Butterfly Association — Porterweed as Nectar Plant

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; gymnema is a woody climbing vine from the tropical dry forests of India and Sri Lanka — famous for its ability to temporarily suppress the sweet taste: chewing fresh leaves makes sweet foods taste like they have no sweetness at all for 1–2 hours; used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 2,000 years to treat diabetes and sugar cravings, and now clinically studied for blood sugar management.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; provide a trellis or support structure; propagate by stem cuttings. Gymnema sylvestre (gymnema, sugar destroyer, or gurmar — Hindi for 'destroyer of sugar') is a woody climbing vine native to the tropical dry forests of central and southern India and Sri Lanka, famous in both traditional medicine and modern nutritional science for one of the most remarkable food interactions known in botany. The fresh leaves of gymnema contain gymnemic acids — triterpene saponins that temporarily bind to and block the sweet taste receptors on the tongue — causing a complete, reversible suppression of the ability to taste sweetness for 1–2 hours after the leaves are chewed. After chewing gymnema leaf, eating sugar tastes like sand, honey tastes like nothing, chocolate loses all sweetness, and sweet fruits taste only of their sour or astringent components — a dramatic, repeatable, pharmacological food interaction. This property has given gymnema both its ancient role in Ayurvedic medicine (used for 2,000+ years for 'sweet urine' — now understood as diabetes) and modern research interest: clinical studies suggest gymnemic acids may reduce sugar absorption in the intestine and support healthy blood glucose management. Gymnema is now one of the most widely sold botanical supplements for blood sugar support worldwide. In zone 9–12, it grows as an ornamental vine with elliptical leaves and small, yellow, tubular flowers.
sources: Journal of Clinical Biochemistry and Nutrition — Gymnema Clinical Studies

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after soil warms (68°F+) in moist to wet, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, clay to loamy soil in full sun; prickly sesban (dhaincha) is one of the most important green manure and cover crop plants of tropical and subtropical rice farming systems — extraordinarily fast-growing (up to 1 inch per day), nitrogen-fixing (80–120 lbs N/acre), and tolerant of waterlogged, flooded, and saline soils that most cover crops cannot handle.

directions: Broadcast or drill at 20–30 lbs/acre in full sun in moist to waterlogged conditions; incorporate or chop-and-drop before seed set to prevent naturalization; do not allow to seed down in sensitive wetland areas. Sesbania bispinosa (prickly sesban or dhaincha) is one of the most important green manure and cover crop species in tropical Asian rice farming systems — a fast-growing, nitrogen-fixing annual legume widely used across India, Bangladesh, Thailand, and the Philippines as a pre-rice or in-rice green manure crop. The plant's growth speed is remarkable: under warm, wet conditions it can add over an inch of height per day, reaching 4–8 feet in just 60–75 days from seeding. Its nitrogen fixation is similarly impressive, with reported values of 80–120 lbs of N per acre in a single growing cycle — equivalent to substantial synthetic fertilizer applications. Prickly sesban is uniquely adapted to waterlogged and flooded conditions that most cover crops cannot tolerate — making it the standard choice for pre-flooding cover crops in paddy rice systems, where it can be grown, then incorporated into the field before flooding for transplanted rice. It also tolerates moderately saline soils better than most legumes. The 'prickly' in the name refers to spines on the seed pods. In zone 8–12, dhaincha is an excellent summer cover crop for warm, moist gardens; cut before flowering to use as a soil amendment.
sources: USDA SARE — Cover Crop Encyclopedia: Sesbania
International Rice Research Institute (IRRI) — Green Manures for Rice
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cover Crops for the Tropics

common_name: Spanish Cherry (Bakul, Bullet Wood, Tanjong Tree)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy soil in full sun; Spanish cherry (bakul) is a slow-growing, long-lived tropical evergreen tree producing exceptionally fragrant, small white flowers used in Indian garlands and hair oil — the intensely sweet, jasmine-like fragrance of the dried flowers intensifies as they dry; the olive-sized, orange-red fruits are sweet-acid and edible.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy soil in full sun; Spanish cherry is slow-growing but long-lived and very low-maintenance once established. Mimusops elengi (Spanish cherry, bakul, or tanjong tree) is a slow-growing, long-lived tropical evergreen tree native to India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, and the Pacific Islands — one of the most beloved ornamental and fragrance trees of the Indian subcontinent. The tree produces small (1/2-inch), white, star-shaped flowers with deeply fringed petals that emit an extraordinarily intense, sweet, jasmine-like fragrance — particularly at night. Remarkably, the fragrance of the flowers intensifies dramatically as they dry: fallen bakul flowers can perfume an entire courtyard for days after dropping, and dried bakul flowers are strung into garlands, threaded into women's hair (a classic Indian tradition), used in temple offerings, and dried for pot-pourri and perfume production. The sweet-acid, olive-sized, orange-red to orange fruits (the 'Spanish cherry') are edible — sweet, slightly astringent, with a pleasant, mild flavor — eaten fresh or used in preserves across the plant's native range. The hard, dense wood (bullet wood) is extremely durable. In zone 10–12 tropical gardens, bakul is an outstanding shade tree and long-term ornamental investment valued as much for its fragrance as its appearance.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Mimusops elengi
National Botanical Research Institute India — Bakul Tree
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Yellow Bauhinia (Bell Bauhinia, St. Thomas Tree)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; yellow bauhinia is a deciduous to semi-evergreen shrub or small tree from tropical Africa and India — producing large (3–4 inch), distinctive bell-shaped, pale-yellow flowers with a dark maroon to near-black stain on one petal; different from the more commonly grown orchid trees (B. variegata and B. purpurea) in its smaller stature and unique yellow-and-dark-red flower color combination.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape. Bauhinia tomentosa (yellow bauhinia, bell bauhinia, or St. Thomas tree) is a deciduous to semi-evergreen shrub or small tree from the tropical dry forests and savannas of East Africa, India, and Sri Lanka — a member of the enormous Bauhinia genus (orchid trees) but markedly different in appearance from the more commonly cultivated purple orchid tree (B. variegata) and red orchid tree (B. blakeana). The flowers of B. tomentosa are distinctive and elegant: large (3–4 inch), bell-shaped with five petals that do not spread as broadly as other orchid tree flowers, colored a soft pale-yellow to cream-yellow with one petal bearing a dramatic deep-maroon to near-black central stain — a striking bicolor combination unlike any other Bauhinia. The leaves are the characteristic twin-lobed 'butterfly' shape shared by all Bauhinia species (resembling two joined leaves — the genus name honors botanist Gaspard and Johann Bauhin, twin brothers, for this two-lobed leaf). In zone 8–11, yellow bauhinia is a more compact and cold-hardy Bauhinia option than many tropical relatives, making it useful in zone 8b gardens that cannot support larger tropical Bauhinia species. The bark has traditional medicinal uses in Indian Ayurveda.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Bauhinia tomentosa
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
